Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
Ensemble Techniques and Model Tuning
Visa Approval Facilitation

Problem Statement¶

Context¶

Business communities in the United States are facing high demand for human resources, but one of the constant challenges is identifying and attracting the right talent, which is perhaps the most important element in remaining competitive. Companies in the United States look for hard-working, talented, and qualified individuals both locally as well as abroad.

The Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) of the US permits foreign workers to come to the United States to work on either a temporary or permanent basis. The act also protects US workers against adverse impacts on their wages or working conditions by ensuring US employers' compliance with statutory requirements when they hire foreign workers to fill workforce shortages. The immigration programs are administered by the Office of Foreign Labor Certification (OFLC).

OFLC processes job certification applications for employers seeking to bring foreign workers into the United States and grants certifications in those cases where employers can demonstrate that there are not sufficient US workers available to perform the work at wages that meet or exceed the wage paid for the occupation in the area of intended employment.

Objective¶

In FY 2016, the OFLC processed 775,979 employer applications for 1,699,957 positions for temporary and permanent labor certifications. This was a nine percent increase in the overall number of processed applications from the previous year. The process of reviewing every case is becoming a tedious task as the number of applicants is increasing every year.

The increasing number of applicants every year calls for a Machine Learning based solution that can help in shortlisting the candidates having higher chances of VISA approval. OFLC has hired the firm EasyVisa for data-driven solutions. You as a data scientist at EasyVisa have to analyze the data provided and, with the help of a classification model:

  • Facilitate the process of visa approvals.
  • Recommend a suitable profile for the applicants for whom the visa should be certified or denied based on the drivers that significantly influence the case status.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of employee and the employer. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

  • case_id: ID of each visa application
  • continent: Information of continent the employee
  • education_of_employee: Information of education of the employee
  • has_job_experience: Does the employee has any job experience? Y= Yes; N = No
  • requires_job_training: Does the employee require any job training? Y = Yes; N = No
  • no_of_employees: Number of employees in the employer's company
  • yr_of_estab: Year in which the employer's company was established
  • region_of_employment: Information of foreign worker's intended region of employment in the US.
  • prevailing_wage: Average wage paid to similarly employed workers in a specific occupation in the area of intended employment. The purpose of the prevailing wage is to ensure that the foreign worker is not underpaid compared to other workers offering the same or similar service in the same area of employment.
  • unit_of_wage: Unit of prevailing wage. Values include Hourly, Weekly, Monthly, and Yearly.
  • full_time_position: Is the position of work full-time? Y = Full Time Position; N = Part Time Position
  • case_status: Flag indicating if the Visa was certified or denied

Note: This is a sample solution for the project. Projects will NOT be graded on the basis of how well the submission matches this sample solution. Projects will be graded on the basis of the rubric only.¶

import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

#import librarires for data access nd manipulation
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

#import libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

#import libraries to split data
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# To oversample and undersample data
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from imblearn. under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score

# Removes the limit for the number of displayed columns
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
# Sets the limit for the number of displayed rows
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 100)

# Libraries different ensemble classifiers
from sklearn.ensemble import (
  BaggingClassifier,
  RandomForestClassifier,
  AdaBoostClassifier,
  GradientBoostingClassifier
)

# To tune different models
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV

from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

# Libraries to get different metric scores
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.metrics import (
  confusion_matrix,
  accuracy_score,
  precision_score,
  recall_score,
  f1_score
)

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

  • EDA is an important part of any project involving data.
  • It is important to investigate and understand the data better before building a model with it.
  • A few questions have been mentioned below which will help you approach the analysis in the right manner and generate insights from the data.
  • A thorough analysis of the data, in addition to the questions mentioned below, should be done.

Leading Questions

What is the distribution of visa case statuses (certified vs. denied)?

  1. What is the distribution of visa case statuses (certified vs. denied)?
  2. How does the education level of employees impact visa approval rates?
  3. Is there a significant difference in visa approval rates between employees with and without prior job experience?
  4. How does the prevailing wage affect visa approval? Do higher wages lead to higher chances of approval?
  5. Do certain regions in the US have higher visa approval rates compared to others?
  6. How does the number of employees in a company influence visa approval? Do larger companies have a higher approval rate?
  7. Are visa approval rates different across various continents of employees? Which continent has the highest and lowest approval rates?

Observations

  • Each case_id is unique, confirming that every record represents a distinct application.
  • Most applications originate from Asia, followed by Europe and North America.
  • The majority of employees hold a Bachelor’s or Master’s degree, with fewer having High School or Doctorate qualifications.
  • More applicants have prior job experience than those without experience.
  • Most positions do not require job training, indicating a preference for skilled workers.
  • Employment is mainly concentrated in the Northeast, South, and West regions.
  • Wages are predominantly reported on a yearly basis, with other units used infrequently.
  • The vast majority of roles are full-time positions.
  • Certified cases significantly outnumber denied cases, indicating a higher approval rate.

Univariate Analysis¶

In [323]:
def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(15, 10), kde=False, bins=None):
  """
  Boxplot and histogram combined

  data: dataframe
  feature: dataframe column
  figsize: size of figure (default (15,10))
  kde: whether to show the density curve (default False)
  bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
  """

  f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
    nrows=2, # Number of rows of the subplot grid= 2
    sharex=True, # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
    gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
    figsize=figsize
  )
  # creating the 2 subplots
  sns.boxplot(
    data=data, x=feature, ax=ax_box2, showmeans=True, color="violet"
  ) # boxplot will be created and a triangle will indicate the mean value of the column
  sns.histplot(
    data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2, bins=bins
  ) if bins else sns.histplot(
      data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2
  ) # For histogram
  ax_hist2.axvline(
  data[feature].mean(), color="green", linestyle=" -- "
  ) # Add mean to the histogram
  ax_hist2.axvline(
  data[feature].median(), color="black", linestyle="-"
  ) # Add median to the histogram
In [324]:
# function to create labeled barplots

def labeled_barplot(data, feature, perc=False, n=None):
  """
  Barplot with pepcentage at the top

  data: dataframe
  feature: dataframe column
  perc: whether to display percentages instead of count (default is False)
  n: displays the top n category levels (default is None, i.e., display all Levels)
  """
  total = len(data[feature]) # length of the column
  count = data[feature].nunique()
  if n is None:
    plt.figure(figsize=(count + 1, 5))
  else:
    plt.figure(figsize=(n + 1, 5))

  plt.xticks(rotation=90, fontsize=15)
  plt.xticks(rotation=90, fontsize=15)
  ax = sns.countplot (
    data=data,
    x=feature,
    palette="Paired",
    order=data[feature].value_counts().index[:n].sort_values(),
  )

  for p in ax.patches:
    if perc == True:
      label = "{:.1f}%".format(
        100 * p.get_height()/total
      ) # percentage of each class of the category
    else:
      label = p.get_height() # count of each level of the category

    x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2 # width of the plot
    y = p.get_height() # height of the plot

    ax.annotate(
      label,
      (x, y),
      ha="center",
      va="center",
      size=12,
      xytext=(0, 5),
      textcoords="offset points"
    ) # annotate the percentage

  plt.show() # show the plot

Observations on education of employee

In [325]:
labeled_barplot(data, "education_of_employee", perc=True)

The bar chart shows the distribution of employees' education levels in the dataset:

  • The largest group holds a Bachelor’s degree (40.2%).
  • The second largest group has a Master’s degree (37.8%).
  • Employees with a High School education make up 13.4%.
  • Those with a Doctorate degree represent the smallest group at 8.6%.

This confirms that most employees are highly educated, with the majority holding Bachelor’s or Master’s degrees.

Observations on region of employment

In [326]:
labeled_barplot(data, "region_of_employment", perc=True)

The bar chart illustrates the distribution of employment cases by region:

  • The Northeast region has the highest share at 28.2%.
  • The South closely follows with 27.5%.
  • The West region accounts for 25.8% of cases.
  • The Midwest makes up 16.9%.
  • The Island region has the smallest portion, only 1.5%.

This shows that most employment cases are concentrated in the Northeast, South, and West regions, with minimal representation from the Island region.

Observations on job experience

In [327]:
labeled_barplot(data, "has_job_experience", perc=True)

The bar chart shows the distribution of applicants based on job experience:

  • 58.1% of applicants have prior job experience (marked as "Y").
  • 41.9% of applicants do not have job experience (marked as "N").

This indicates that the majority of applicants come with some level of prior job experience.

Observations on case status

In [328]:
labeled_barplot(data, "case_status", perc=True)
  • Majority of cases are Certified (66.8%), indicating approvals are more common than denials.
  • Denied cases account for 33.2%, representing a significant portion of total cases.
  • Certification rate is approximately twice the denial rate.
  • Overall outcomes suggest a generally favorable approval trend.
  • The noticeable share of denials highlights the need for further analysis of rejection factors.

Bivariate Analysis¶

In [329]:
# defining the size of the plot
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
num_features = data.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64']).columns.tolist()

# plotting the heatmap for correlation
sns.heatmap(
    data[num_features].corr(),annot=True, vmin=-1, vmax=1, fmt=".2f", cmap="Spectral"
);
  • Very weak correlations among all variables, with coefficients close to zero.
  • Number of employees has a negligible negative correlation with year of establishment (-0.02).
  • Number of employees and prevailing wage are almost uncorrelated (-0.01).
  • Year of establishment shows a very slight positive correlation with prevailing wage (0.01).
  • Overall, the variables appear largely independent, indicating no strong linear relationships.
In [330]:
def stacked_barplot(data, predictor, target):
  """
  data: input dataset
  predictor: independent variable
  target: target variable
  """
  count = data[predictor].nunique()
  sorter = data[target].value_counts().index[-1]

  # creating a crosstab for predictor vs target
  tab1 = pd.crosstab(
      data[predictor],
      data[target],
      margins=True,
  ).sort_values(by=sorter, ascending=False)

  print(tab1)
  print("-" * 120)


  tab = pd.crosstab(
      data[predictor],
      data[target],
      normalize='index'    # normalizing by dividing each row by its row total
  ).sort_values(by=sorter, ascending=False)    # sorting the resulting crosstab


  # Plot the stacked bar chart
  tab.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, figsize=(7, 5))    # creating a stacked bar chart from the normalized crosstab
  plt.xlabel(predictor)
  plt.legend(loc='upper left', bbox_to_anchor=(1, 1), title=target);    # adding a legend for the 'Approval' column
In [331]:
### function to plot distributions wrt target

def distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, predictor, target):

  fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))

  target_uniq = data[target].unique()

  axs[0, 0].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[0]))
  sns.histplot(
    data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[0]],
    x=predictor,
    kde=True,
    ax=axs[0, 0],
    color="teal",
    stat="density",
  )

  axs [0, 1].set_title("Distribution of target for target=" + str(target_uniq[1]))
  sns.histplot(
    data=data[data[target] == target_uniq[1]],
    x=predictor,
    kde=True,
    ax=axs[0, 1],
    color="orange",
    stat="density",
  )

  axs[1, 0].set_title("Boxplot w.r.t target")
  sns.boxplot(data=data, x=target, y=predictor, ax=axs[1, 0], palette="gist_rainbow")


  axs [1, 1].set_title("Boxplot (without outliers) w.r.t target")
  sns.boxplot(
    data=data,
    x=target,
    y=predictor,
    ax=axs[1, 1],
    showfliers=False,
    palette="gist_rainbow"
  )

  plt.tight_layout()
  plt.show()
In [332]:
stacked_barplot(data, "education_of_employee", "case_status")

case_status            Certified  Denied    All
education_of_employee                          
All                        17018    8462  25480
Bachelor's                  6367    3867  10234
High School                 1164    2256   3420
Master's                    7575    2059   9634
Doctorate                   1912     280   2192
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  • Certification rates increase with higher education levels.
  • Doctorate holders show the highest certification proportion and the lowest denial rate.
  • Master’s degree holders also have a strong likelihood of certification, though slightly lower than doctorates.
  • Bachelor’s degree holders have a more balanced split, with a noticeable share of denials.
  • High school level cases have the highest denial rate, exceeding certifications.
  • Overall, the chart suggests a positive relationship between education level and approval likelihood.
In [333]:
stacked_barplot1 = stacked_barplot(data, "continent", "case_status")

case_status    Certified  Denied    All
continent                              
All                17018    8462  25480
Asia               11012    5849  16861
North America       2037    1255   3292
Europe              2957     775   3732
South America        493     359    852
Africa               397     154    551
Oceania              122      70    192
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Here are some clear takeaways from the chart:

  • Europe has the highest certification rate (~80%) and the lowest denial rate, standing out from the rest.
  • Africa follows Europe, with a relatively high certification share (~72%) compared to other regions.
  • Asia and Oceania sit in the middle, with certification rates around the mid-60% range.
  • North America is slightly lower than Asia/Oceania, with a noticeable denial portion.
  • South America has the lowest certification rate (~58%) and the highest denial rate among all continents.
  • Overall trend: Certification rates generally increase from South America → Europe, while denial rates decrease accordingly.
In [334]:
stacked_barplot(data, "has_job_experience", "case_status")

case_status         Certified  Denied    All
has_job_experience                          
All                     17018    8462  25480
N                        5994    4684  10678
Y                       11024    3778  14802
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Here are the key observations from this chart:

  • Applicants with job experience (“Y”) have a much higher certification rate (~74%) compared to those without experience.
  • Applicants without job experience (“N”) face a higher denial rate, with denials making up nearly 44% of cases.
  • Job experience appears to be a strong positive factor in certification outcomes.
  • The gap between certified and denied cases is significantly wider for experienced applicants than for inexperienced ones.

In short: having job experience greatly improves the likelihood of certification.

In [335]:
# Prevailing wages based on region of employment
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=data, x='region_of_employment', y='prevailing_wage');

Here are the main observations from the boxplot:

  • Midwest and Island regions have the highest median prevailing wages, indicating generally better pay levels in these regions.
  • West and Northeast show lower median wages, with more concentration in the lower-to-mid salary range.
  • South sits in between, with a moderate median wage compared to other regions.
  • All regions show wide wage dispersion, suggesting substantial variability in prevailing wages regardless of region.
  • High-end outliers are present across all regions, with some wages exceeding $300,000, especially in the Midwest.
  • The Midwest displays the widest spread, indicating a mix of both lower- and higher-paying roles.

Overall, wage levels vary notably by region, with Midwest and Island offering higher typical wages, while West and Northeast trend lower.

In [336]:
distribution_plot_wrt_target(data, "prevailing_wage", "case_status")
  • Both Denied and Certified prevailing wage distributions are right-skewed with long upper tails
  • Majority of wages fall in the $30k–$120k range for both outcomes
  • Certified cases tend to have higher prevailing wages overall
  • Density for Certified is higher in the mid-to-high wage range (~$60k–$120k)
  • Denied cases show more concentration at lower wages, especially below ~$50k
  • Median prevailing wage is higher for Certified than Denied
  • Certified cases have a slightly wider IQR, indicating greater wage variability
  • Many high-wage outliers exist in both categories
  • After removing outliers, Certified still shows higher median and quartiles
  • Indicates prevailing wage is positively associated with certification, but with significant overlap between outcomes
In [337]:
stacked_barplot(data, "unit_of_wage", "case_status")

case_status   Certified  Denied    All
unit_of_wage                          
All               17018    8462  25480
Year              16047    6915  22962
Hour                747    1410   2157
Week                169     103    272
Month                55      34     89
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  • Hourly wages have the highest denial rate, with Denied cases exceeding Certified
  • Monthly, weekly, and yearly wages show higher certification rates than denials
  • Yearly wage unit has the highest proportion of Certified cases
  • Certification rate increases as the wage unit becomes longer-term (Hour → Year)
  • Hourly wage filings appear riskier or more scrutinized compared to salaried filings
  • Salaried structures (month/year) are more commonly associated with certification
  • Indicates that unit_of_wage is a strong categorical indicator for case outcome

Data Pre-processing¶

  • Missing value treatment (if needed)
  • Feature engineering (if needed)
  • Outlier detection and treatment (if needed)
  • Preparing data for modeling
  • Any other preprocessing steps (if needed)
In [338]:
#outlier detection

numeric_columns = data.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64']).columns.tolist()

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 12))

for i, column in enumerate(numeric_columns, 1):
    plt.subplot(4,4, i + 1)
    plt.boxplot(data[column])
    plt.title(column)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

  • Number of employees is extremely right-skewed with many small firms and a few very large employers

  • Presence of numerous extreme outliers in employee count (up to hundreds of thousands)

  • Median employer size is relatively small, despite very large maximum values

  • Year of establishment is concentrated around recent decades

  • Median establishment year is close to 2000, indicating many relatively young firms

  • Older firms (1800s–early 1900s) appear as lower-end outliers

  • Prevailing wage shows strong right skewness

  • Most wages are clustered in the lower-to-mid range, with a long high-wage tail

  • Several high-wage outliers (> $200k) are present

  • Overall, all three variables show non-normal distributions, suggesting the need for log transformation, robust scaling, or outlier handling before modeling

Data preparation for modelling¶

  • Our objective is to predict whether a visa application will be certified
  • Before building the model, we will encode categorical features
  • We will then split the dataset into training and testing sets to evaluate the model’s performance on unseen data
In [339]:
data["case_status"] = data["case_status"].apply(lambda x: 1 if x == "Certified" else 0)
X = data.drop(["case_status"], axis=1)
y = data["case_status"]

X = pd.get_dummies(X, drop_first=True)
# Splitting data into training and validation set:
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
  X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=1, stratify=y
)

#Test data
X_val,X_test,y_val,y_test = train_test_split(
  X_val, y_val, test_size=0.1,random_state=1,stratify=y_val
)

print("_" * 50)

Model Building¶

Model Evaluation Criterion

A model can make incorrect predictions in two ways:

  1. Predicting that a visa application will be certified when it should actually be denied.
  2. Predicting that a visa application will be denied when it should actually be certified.

Which case is more important?

Both types of errors are equally important:

  • If a visa is certified when it should have been denied, an unsuitable foreign worker may fill the position, potentially reducing job opportunities for U.S. citizens.
  • If a visa is denied when it should have been certified, the U.S. may lose a qualified professional who could contribute positively to the economy.

How do we reduce these losses?

  • We will use the F1-score as the evaluation metric, as it balances precision and recall and helps minimize both false positives and false negatives.
  • Balanced class weights will be applied so the model gives equal importance to both certified and denied cases.

Implementation approach

  • The model_performance_classification_sklearn function will be used to evaluate model performance using multiple classification metrics.
  • The confusion_matrix_sklearn function will be used to visualize and analyze the confusion matrix for each model.

We define a utility function to collate all the metrics into a single data frame, and another to plot the confusion matrix.

In [341]:
# defining a function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model built using sklearn
def model_performance_classification(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    # predicting using the independent variables
    pred = model.predict(predictors)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)  # to compute Recall
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)  # to compute Precision
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)  # to compute F1-score

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1,},
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf
In [342]:
def plot_confusion_matrix(model, predictors, target):
    """
    To plot the confusion_matrix with percentages

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """
    # Predict the target values using the provided model and predictors
    y_pred = model.predict(predictors)

    # Compute the confusion matrix comparing the true target values with the predicted values
    cm = confusion_matrix(target, y_pred)

    # Create labels for each cell in the confusion matrix with both count and percentage
    labels = np.asarray(
        [
            ["{0:0.0f}".format(item) + "\n{0:.2%}".format(item / cm.flatten().sum())]
            for item in cm.flatten()
        ]
    ).reshape(2, 2)    # reshaping to a matrix

    # Set the figure size for the plot
    plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))

    # Plot the confusion matrix as a heatmap with the labels
    sns.heatmap(cm, annot=labels, fmt="")

    # Add a label to the y-axis
    plt.ylabel("True label")

    # Add a label to the x-axis
    plt.xlabel("Predicted label")
In [343]:
#defining scorer
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.f1_score)

Model building with original data

In [344]:
models = [] # Empty list to store all the models

# Appending models into the list
models.append(("Bagging", BaggingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Random forest", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("GB", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Adaboost", AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Xgboost", XGBClassifier(random_state=1, eval_metric="logloss")))
models.append(("dtree", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)))

results = [] # Empty list to store all model's CV scores
names = [] # Empty list to store name of the models

# loop through all models to get the mean cross validated score
print("\n" "Cross-Validation performance on training dataset:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
  kfold = StratifiedKFold(
    n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=1
  ) # Setting number of splits equal to 5
  cv_result = cross_val_score(
    estimator=model, X=X_train, y=y_train, scoring = scorer, cv=kfold
  )

  results.append(cv_result)
  names.append(name)
  print("{}: {}".format(name, cv_result.mean()))

print("\n" "Validation Performance:" "\n")

for name, model in models:
  model.fit(X_train, y_train)
  scores = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))
  print("{}: {}".format(name, scores))

Cross-Validation performance on training dataset:

Bagging: 0.7756586246579394
Random forest: 0.8037837241749051
GB: 0.823039791269532
Adaboost: 0.8203377989495703
Xgboost: 0.8095211182586954
dtree: 0.7410652876513099

Validation Performance:

Bagging: 0.7675817565350541
Random forest: 0.7972364702187794
GB: 0.8195818459969403
Adaboost: 0.8158053488839735
Xgboost: 0.8083918974782968
dtree: 0.7477497255762898
In [345]:
# Plotting boxplots for CV scores of all models defined above

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
fig.suptitle("Cross-validation scores")
ax = fig.add_subplot(111)

plt.boxplot(results)
ax.set_xticklabels(names)

plt.show()
  • Gradient Boosting (GB) shows the highest median performance (~0.82+) among all models.
  • AdaBoost performs very similarly to GB, with a slightly lower median but tight variability, indicating stable results.
  • XGBoost ranks next, with strong performance (~0.81) and moderate consistency.
  • Random Forest performs reasonably well (~0.80) but lags behind boosting methods.
  • Bagging has lower median accuracy (~0.775) and slightly more spread than Random Forest.
  • Decision Tree has the lowest median score (~0.74) and higher variability, indicating poorer and less stable performance.
  • Overall trend: Ensemble boosting methods outperform bagging and single-tree models.
  • Boosting models also show tighter interquartile ranges, suggesting better generalization.

Model Building with oversampled data¶

X_train_over, y_train_over = sm.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

print("After OverSampling, counts of label '1': {}".format(sum(y_train_over == 1)))
print("After OverSampling, counts of label '0': {} \n".format(sum(y_train_over == 0)))

print("After OverSampling, the shape of train_X: {}".format(X_train_over.shape))
print("After OverSampling, the shape of train_y: {} \n".format(y_train_over.shape))

In [348]:
# Plotting boxplots for CV scores of all models defined above

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
fig.suptitle("Cross-validation scores")
ax = fig.add_subplot(111)

plt.boxplot(results)
ax.set_xticklabels(names)

plt.show()
  • Gradient Boosting (GB) achieves the highest median score, indicating the best overall performance.
  • AdaBoost performs very close to GB, with a slightly lower median but consistent results.
  • XGBoost shows strong performance, ranking just below the other boosting methods.
  • Random Forest has moderate accuracy, better than Bagging but weaker than boosting models.
  • Bagging performs lower than Random Forest, with a noticeably smaller median score.
  • Decision Tree (dtree) has the lowest performance and the largest variability, suggesting instability.
  • Overall, boosting-based models outperform bagging and single-tree models.
  • Lower spread in boosting models indicates better generalization across folds.

In [351]:
# Plotting boxplots for CV scores of all models defined above

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
fig.suptitle("Cross-validation scores")
ax = fig.add_subplot(111)

plt.boxplot(results)
ax.set_xticklabels(names)

plt.show()
  • Gradient Boosting (GB) has the highest median score (~0.71–0.72), making it the best-performing model here.
  • XGBoost and AdaBoost show similar median performance (~0.69–0.70), slightly below GB.
  • Random Forest performs moderately well (~0.68–0.69) but trails the boosting methods.
  • Bagging has a lower median score (~0.64) with a relatively tight spread.
  • Decision Tree (dtree) again shows the lowest performance (~0.61–0.62).
  • Outliers are present in most models, especially in GB, AdaBoost, and XGBoost, indicating some fold sensitivity.
  • Boosting methods consistently outperform bagging and single-tree models.
  • Overall performance levels are lower than the previous plot, suggesting a harder dataset or different evaluation setting.

Cross-Comparison Summary¶

  • Best raw performance: Original data
  • Best class balance & fairness: Oversampled data
  • Lowest but unbiased performance: Undersampled data
  • Most robust models across all datasets: Gradient Boosting and XGBoost
  • Least reliable model: Single Decision Tree

While original data yields the highest accuracy, oversampling provides a better trade-off between performance and class balance, whereas undersampling sacrifices accuracy for fairness.

Model Performance Improvement¶

Note¶

  1. Sample parameter grids have been provided to do necessary hyperparameter tuning. These sample grids are expected to provide a balance between model performance improvement and execution time. One can extend/reduce the parameter grid based on execution time and system configuration.
  • Please note that if the parameter grid is extended to improve the model performance further, the execution time will increase
  1. The models chosen in this notebook are based on test runs. One can update the best models as obtained upon code execution and tune them for best performance.
  • For Gradient Boosting:
param_grid = {
    "init": [AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)],
    "n_estimators": np.arange(50,110,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01,0.1,0.05],
    "subsample":[0.7,0.9],
    "max_features":[0.5,0.7,1],
}
  • For Adaboost:
param_grid = {
    "n_estimators": np.arange(50,110,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01,0.1,0.05],
    "base_estimator": [
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=2, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1),
    ],
}
  • For Bagging Classifier:
param_grid = {
    'max_samples': [0.8,0.9,1],
    'max_features': [0.7,0.8,0.9],
    'n_estimators' : [30,50,70],
}
  • For Random Forest:
param_grid = {
    "n_estimators": [50,110,25],
    "min_samples_leaf": np.arange(1, 4),
    "max_features": [np.arange(0.3, 0.6, 0.1),'sqrt'],
    "max_samples": np.arange(0.4, 0.7, 0.1)
}
  • For Decision Trees:
param_grid = {
    'max_depth': np.arange(2,6),
    'min_samples_leaf': [1, 4, 7],
    'max_leaf_nodes' : [10, 15],
    'min_impurity_decrease': [0.0001,0.001]
}
  • For XGBoost:
param_grid={'n_estimators':np.arange(50,110,25),
            'scale_pos_weight':[1,2,5],
            'learning_rate':[0.01,0.1,0.05],
            'gamma':[1,3],
            'subsample':[0.7,0.9]
}
In [363]:

Model Comparison and Final Model Selection¶

In [364]:
# training performance comparison

models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
  [
  gbm_train_perf.T,
  xgb_train_perf.T,
  ada_train_perf.T,
  random_forest_train_perf.T,
  ],
  axis=1,
)

models_train_comp_df.columns = [
"Gradient Boosting tuned with oversampled data",
"XGBoost tuned with oversampled data",
"AdaBoost tuned with oversampled data",
"Random forest tuned with undersampled data",
]

print("Training performance comparison:")

models_train_comp_df

Training performance comparison:
Out[364]:
Gradient Boosting tuned with oversampled data XGBoost tuned with oversampled data AdaBoost tuned with oversampled data Random forest tuned with undersampled data
Accuracy 0.816209 0.808654 0.793839 0.842141
Recall 0.859481 1.000000 0.839419 0.842985
Precision 0.791023 0.723227 0.769290 0.841564
F1 0.823832 0.839387 0.802826 0.842274
In [365]:
# validation performance comparison

models_val_comp_df = pd.concat(
  [
  gbm_val_perf.T,
  xgb_val_perf.T,
  ada_val_perf.T,
  random_forest_val_perf.T,
  ],
  axis=1,
)

models_val_comp_df.columns = [
"Gradient Boosting tuned with oversampled data",
"XGBoost tuned with oversampled data",
"AdaBoost tuned with oversampled data",
"Random forest tuned with undersampled data",
]

print("Validation performance comparison:")

models_val_comp_df

Validation performance comparison:
Out[365]:
Gradient Boosting tuned with oversampled data XGBoost tuned with oversampled data AdaBoost tuned with oversampled data Random forest tuned with undersampled data
Accuracy 0.711586 0.697340 0.734118 0.699520
Recall 0.818894 0.968872 0.835002 0.709621
Precision 0.765568 0.696557 0.781740 0.816429
F1 0.791334 0.810452 0.807494 0.759287
  • Random Forest (undersampled data) achieves the highest accuracy and F1-score in the first evaluation, indicating strong overall balance.
  • Boosting models trained on oversampled data show higher recall, especially XGBoost.
  • XGBoost (oversampled) achieves very high recall (≈1.0) in both evaluations but suffers from low precision, leading to more false positives.
  • Gradient Boosting (oversampled) shows stable and consistent performance, with good recall and balanced precision.
  • AdaBoost (oversampled) provides the best precision–recall balance among boosting models.
  • In the second evaluation, all models experience performance degradation, indicating a harder test set or reduced generalization.
  • Oversampling improves minority class detection but can reduce precision.
  • Undersampling improves precision and accuracy but may reduce recall due to information loss.
  • Overall, XGBoost is recall-oriented, Random Forest is precision-oriented, and AdaBoost is the most balanced choice.

Now we have our final model, let's see how it performs on test data

In [366]:
ada_test = model_performance_classification(tuned_ada, X_test, y_test)
ada_test
Out[366]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.752941 0.849315 0.794872 0.821192
Feature Importance¶
In [367]:
# importance of features in the tree building
feature_names = X_train.columns
importances = tuned_ada.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color="violet", align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

Actionable Insights¶

  • Applicants with prior job experience have significantly higher visa approval rates.
  • Lack of experience is strongly associated with higher denial rates.
  • Prevailing wage is positively correlated with approval — higher wages improve chances.
  • Employer size matters — larger companies tend to receive more certifications.
  • Regional differences exist — some U.S. regions show consistently higher approval rates.
  • The dataset shows a substantial class imbalance, with certifications dominating denials.
  • Key predictive features include job experience, wage, employer size, and region.

Recommendations¶

  • Use job experience as a primary filter in applicant pre-screening.
  • Encourage employers to offer competitive wages aligned with regional market standards.
  • Prioritize applications from established organizations with proven approval history.
  • Incorporate regional weighting in the ML model to reflect approval patterns.
  • Deploy the ML classifier as a decision-support tool to rank applicants by approval probability.
  • Continuously retrain the model with new data to adapt to policy or labor market changes.
  • Provide feedback to applicants on key improvement areas (experience, wage level, employer type).
  • Use model outputs for workload reduction, allowing officers to focus on borderline cases.
